entries.title: Game Theory Nash Equilibrium
entries.content: A stable state of a game where no player has an incentive to unilaterally change their strategy.
entries.example: Prisoner's Dilemma both confessing

entries.title: Gamma Distribution
entries.content: A two-parameter family of continuous probability distributions, generalizing the exponential distribution.
entries.example: Modeling service times or rainfall amounts

entries.title: Gamma Function
entries.content: An extension of the factorial function to complex and real arguments.
entries.example: Gamma(1/2) = sqrt(pi)

entries.title: Gaussian Distribution
entries.content: The normal distribution, characterized by its bell-shaped curve.
entries.example: IQ scores distribution in a population

entries.title: Gaussian Elimination
entries.content: An algorithm for solving systems of linear equations by transforming the augmented matrix into row echelon form.
entries.example: Solving 3 equations with 3 variables using matrices

entries.title: Gaussian Integers
entries.content: Complex numbers whose real and imaginary parts are both integers.
entries.formula: Z[i] = {a + b*i : a, b in Z}
entries.example: 3 + 2i is a Gaussian integer

entries.title: Gauss-Jordan Elimination
entries.content: An extension of Gaussian elimination that transforms a matrix into reduced row echelon form.
entries.example: Finding the inverse of a matrix using row operations

entries.title: Gauss's Law Divergence
entries.content: Physical law relating electrical charge to divergence of electric field, written in vector calculus.
entries.example: Calculating flux through a sphere enclosing charge

entries.title: General Form of Line
entries.content: A linear equation represented in standard form with integer coefficients.
entries.example: Equation of line: 2x - 3y + 6 = 0

entries.title: General Solution of ODE
entries.content: The solution of a differential equation containing arbitrary constants, representing all possible solutions.
entries.example: Solving y'' - 5y' + 6y = 0

entries.title: Generating Function
entries.content: A formal power series whose coefficients encode information about a sequence of numbers.
entries.example: Generating function for Fibonacci numbers

entries.title: Generator of Group
entries.content: An element of a group that can generate all other elements of the group through repeated operations.
entries.example: 1 is a generator of the group of integers modulo 5 under addition

entries.title: Geometric Mean
entries.content: The nth root of the product of n numbers, useful for compounding rates.
entries.example: Geometric mean of 2 and 8 is sqrt(16) = 4

entries.title: Geometric Progression
entries.content: A sequence of numbers where each term after the first is found by multiplying the previous term by a non-zero constant ratio.
entries.example: Sequence: 2, 6, 18, 54... with r = 3

entries.title: Geometric Series
entries.content: The sum of the terms of a geometric progression sequence.

entries.title: Geometry Euclid Axioms
entries.content: The core geometric assumptions formulated by Euclid in his work 'Elements'.
entries.formula: A straight line segment can be drawn joining any two points
entries.example: Axioms defining flat 2D geometry space

entries.title: Girth of Graph
entries.content: The length of a shortest cycle contained in a graph.
entries.example: The girth of a square graph is 4

entries.title: Global Maximum
entries.content: The highest value that a function achieves over its entire domain.
entries.formula: f(c) >= f(x) for all x in domain
entries.example: Global maximum of y = -x^2 is 0 at x = 0

entries.title: Global Minimum
entries.content: The lowest value that a function achieves over its entire domain.
entries.formula: f(c) <= f(x) for all x in domain
entries.example: Global minimum of y = (x-2)^2 + 3 is 3 at x = 2

entries.title: Golden Ratio
entries.content: A mathematical constant ratio of two quantities where their ratio is the same as the ratio of their sum to the larger quantity.
entries.example: phi approx 1.61803

entries.title: Golomb Ruler
entries.content: A set of marks at integer positions along a ruler such that no two pairs of marks are the same distance apart.
entries.example: Ruler [0, 1, 3] with distances 1, 2, 3

entries.title: Googol and Googolplex
entries.content: Googol is 10 raised to the power of 100; Googolplex is 10 raised to the power of a Googol.
entries.example: Extremely large numbers used in combinatorics and cosmology

entries.title: Gradient Descent
entries.content: An optimization algorithm used to minimize a function by iteratively moving in the direction of steepest descent.
entries.example: Training machine learning algorithms to minimize error

entries.title: Gradient Vector
entries.content: A vector of partial derivatives pointing in the direction of the greatest rate of increase of a function.
entries.example: Gradient of f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2 is [2x, 2y]

entries.title: Graph Chromatic Number
entries.content: The smallest number of colors needed to color the vertices of a graph so that no two adjacent vertices share a color.
entries.example: Chromatic number of a triangle graph is 3

entries.title: Graph Cycle
entries.content: A path in a graph starting and ending at the same vertex, with no other repeated vertices.
entries.example: A closed loop path in a network

entries.title: Graph Degree Sequence
entries.content: The list of vertex degrees of a graph in non-increasing order.
entries.example: Degree sequence of a triangle is (2, 2, 2)

entries.title: Graph Edge Coloring
entries.content: An assignment of colors to the edges of a graph such that no two adjacent edges share the same color.
entries.example: Scheduling matches in a round-robin tournament

entries.title: Graph Isomorphism
entries.content: A bijection between the vertex sets of two graphs that preserves adjacency.
entries.example: Checking if two networks are structurally identical

entries.title: Graph Path
entries.content: A sequence of edges which connect a sequence of vertices in a graph.
entries.example: Routing a packet from source to destination

entries.title: Graph Vertex Coloring
entries.content: An assignment of colors to vertices of a graph such that no adjacent vertices share the same color.
entries.example: Coloring regions of a map using at most 4 colors

entries.title: Graph Vertices and Edges
entries.content: The primary components of a graph: vertices are points, and edges are the lines connecting them.
entries.example: Vertices representing cities, edges representing roads

entries.title: Gravitational Constant Formula
entries.content: Formula for the force of gravity between two masses, involving the gravitational constant G.
entries.example: Gravity force between Earth and an apple

entries.title: Greatest Common Divisor
entries.content: The largest positive integer that divides two or more integers without leaving a remainder.

entries.title: Greatest Lower Bound
entries.content: The infimum, which is the greatest element that is less than or equal to all elements of a set.
entries.example: Greatest lower bound of (0, 5) is 0

entries.title: Green's Theorem
entries.content: Relates a line integral around a simple closed curve C to a double integral over the plane region D bounded by C.
entries.formula: contour_integral (P dx + Q dy) = double_integral (dQ/dx - dP/dy) dA
entries.example: Calculating area of a polygon using path integrals

entries.title: Group Axioms
entries.content: A set of four conditions that a set with a binary operation must satisfy to be a group.
entries.example: The set of integers under addition forms a group

entries.title: Group Homomorphism
entries.content: A map between two groups that preserves the group operation.
entries.example: The log map mapping positive reals under multiplication to reals under addition

entries.title: Group Order
entries.content: The number of elements in a group, or the smallest positive integer n such that g^n = e for an element.
entries.example: The cyclic group Z_6 has order 6

entries.title: Gnomonic Projection
entries.content: A map projection that projects points on a sphere onto a tangent plane, drawing great circles as straight lines.
entries.example: Navigation maps for pilots plotting straight flight paths
